ranked_opportunities.role: Kepler Communications - Director of Product - Platform, Payload & Services
ranked_opportunities.verdict: This role fails on two hard blockers. First, the JD explicitly states "Lead product strategy for ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance) use cases...enabling real-time data relay, tasking, and delivery for defense and commercial customers." Paul's standing exclusions include "Defense, IC, or national security roles requiring clearance," and ISR is definitionally defense-focused work. Second, the role requires "8+ years of experience in Product Management, with at least 3+ years in a leadership role." Paul has 15 years building commercial functions and shaping product strategy from the market side, but no formal product management career path with portfolio roadmap ownership, quarterly planning cycles, or engineering-led cross-functional leadership at this scale. The RF cap fires at 4 (unmet domain requirement), IL at 3 (unmet credential). The company stage (33 satellites launched, scaling globally, expanding product leadership) scores SA 6, operational scale, not new-build. SPM 7 reflects platform leverage and multi-stakeholder coordination across government/commercial/defense sectors, which maps to Paul's deal architecture pattern, but not enough to override the dual hard blockers.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based vegetation management for power utilities. Climate resilience meets boring infrastructure money.
watch_list.roast: Amsterdam-based company that uses satellites to tell power companies which trees are about to commit murder on their power lines. The most boringly genius business model in climate tech: utilities have unlimited budgets for 'please stop California from catching fire,' and Overstory cashes those checks with a straight face. Remote-possible, real recurring revenue, and the kind of company where your EO background meets actual money. Not 'potential TAM' money. Actual checks-clearing money.

watch_list.why: Science-based carbon removal verification. Like Verra but for permanent removal, not offsets.
watch_list.roast: Verra's angry younger sibling who thinks everyone else is too soft on carbon credits. They verify permanent carbon removal ,  the 'literally buried it underground for a thousand years' kind, not the 'we planted a sapling and called it a day' kind. Stripe and Frontier money keeps the lights on, and their methodology team has more PhDs per square foot than most MIT labs. Your regulatory background ,  writing actual carbon laws before most of them had an undergrad thesis ,  makes you the adult in every room they put you in. They desperately need someone commercial who speaks 'science' without flinching. You do.

watch_list.why: The OG daily imagery company. Always hiring. You probably know people there already.
watch_list.roast: You negotiated the 1.2 billion hectare coastal coverage deal with Planet in 2023, three parties, five months, 95% discount off commercial rates, and now Planet is restricting imagery access over Iran and the Middle East and the entire maritime intelligence sector is scrambling for alternatives. Bloomberg documented who is affected, commodity traders, energy firms, OSINT researchers, humanitarian organizations, and every one of them is now looking at European and Chinese EO providers because a single US government decision just made Planet unreliable for any use case that touches a conflict zone. Planet's commercial revenue share dropped from 45% to 18% over four years and now the same government relationships that dominate the revenue base are actively degrading the commercial business that remains. You know this pattern, you lived it at KSAT when Norwegian export controls meant you could not sell to certain customers and the customer went to MDA instead, and the lesson is that when your biggest customer can turn your product off for reasons you do not control your commercial business is not a business it is a subsidy with terms and conditions. Planet is hiring, they always are, but the question is whether you want to build a commercial function for a company whose product availability is a political decision, or whether you want to build it for someone whose product is actually theirs to sell. The deal you built with them is still running, the imagery is still processing, and Blue Planet III is still going to feature it, but that deal worked because you structured it as non-commercial with high latency and you knew the competitive harm objection was resolvable because the use cases were structurally different. That clarity does not exist anymore, and if you are Planet's VP of Partnerships in 2026 you are spending half your time explaining to customers why they cannot have the thing they are paying for. That is not a job, that is a hostage negotiation, and you are too old for that.

watch_list.why: Constellation of weather balloons collecting atmospheric data. Paul's NewSpace + climate crossover.
watch_list.roast: Windborne launched 150 weather balloons in 2025 and they want to fly a thousand by 2027. The pitch is simple. Satellites see the top of the atmosphere but they miss what is happening at altitude where the weather actually forms. Radiosondes are sparse and expensive. Windborne's balloons are cheaper, fly longer, and sample the atmosphere continuously as they drift. The data goes to NOAA, ECMWF, and a handful of reinsurers and energy traders who pay for better forecasts. The company raised a Series B in 2024 and they are hiring a Head of BD, which means someone finally realized that atmospheric data has commercial value beyond government contracts. Paul sold KSAT's environmental monitoring into EMSA and AMSA when no one believed governments would pay for proactive surveillance. Windborne is earlier in that curve but the pattern is identical. The buyers are there. They just do not know they are buyers yet. The role is building the commercial function from scratch at a company with real satellites, real customers, and a clear path to revenue. The risk is Windborne's balloons are consumable hardware with per-unit economics that matter, and Paul has never sold a product with a cost-of-goods problem. The opportunity is he has spent twenty years explaining why organizations should pay for data they can technically live without, and Windborne's entire business model depends on that argument working at scale. If he can explain to Exxon why weekly monitoring of the Kara Sea is worth the contract, he can explain to a reinsurer why better hurricane track forecasts three days earlier are worth paying Windborne instead of just waiting for NOAA.

watch_list.why: Weather intelligence platform with own radar satellites. Commercial BD team is substantial.
watch_list.roast: Israeli founders, Boston HQ, their own radar satellites in LEO, and the sort of founding-team conviction that makes 'we're going to be the Bloomberg Terminal of weather' sound like a mission statement and not a term-sheet slide. They sell forecasts to Delta and United when a hurricane is threading the Caribbean, to Walmart when a supply chain is about to get picked up by a twister, and to the Air Force when the stakes are whatever classified-adjacent thing the Air Force does before breakfast. Their BD pipeline is a chef's-knife tour of enterprise: airlines, retailers, logistics majors, defense, insurance, utilities, and the one common thread is that weather surprise costs real money. Your KSAT years were the same work in a different uniform: sell the data to enterprises with operational exposure, build the commercial relationship before the storm not after, let the engineers argue about bands and cadence in a different room. Tomorrow.io is a Patriots-friendly commute from wherever you pick as a landing zone, their satellites actually launch, and Boston in April is close enough to Fenway that Tolle's debut isn't a YouTube stream.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based ag MRV platform. Soil carbon, sustainability reporting, EUDR. Direct domain overlap.
watch_list.roast: They measure dirt from space and sell the results to food companies who need proof they're not destroying civilization. Soil carbon, sustainability reporting, EUDR compliance ,  every European bureaucracy bingo square is a revenue line, and Brussels just punted EUDR another year, which means every Regrow customer just bought twelve more months of breathing room and Regrow just bought twelve more months of runway to convert pilots into multi-year MSAs. The customer list reads like the produce aisle of a high-end Whole Foods: General Mills, Cargill, ADM, every European retailer with a sustainability disclosure obligation, and the agriculture banks that finance them. Soil carbon MRV is finally hitting the curve where the marketing budget moves to the procurement budget, which means contracts get signed instead of conferences attended, and the BD lead actually retires the quota. Your carbon market plus satellite plus regulatory pattern is so comically aligned that their ATS might flag your application as 'too good to be real, probably a bot.' Apply anyway. Make the robot question its algorithms.

watch_list.why: Spanish EO startup, $130M Series B (Apr 2026) led by Nazca Capital. Seven years in stealth, moving to commercialization. L3Harris sensor partnership. Direct match for commercial EO data licensing leadership.
watch_list.roast: Spanish EO startup that just raised 130 million dollars in Series B to map the Earth for AI, which is venture-speak for we have a product that sounds like the future and investors who believe the hype. They also just announced a contract with L3Harris to build sensors for their constellation, which means they have moved from fundraising mode to actually-building-satellites mode. You know this phase. It is the moment when a company realizes that selling the idea of a constellation is easier than selling the data the constellation will produce. Xoople is betting that AI companies will pay for training data, which is a reasonable bet except that the AI companies have already figured out they can scrape Sentinel and Landsat for free, and the marginal value of higher-resolution commercial data is unclear until you can prove it improves model performance. The commercial challenge is not the technology. The challenge is finding the buyers who will pay for the data before the use case is proven, which is the same problem every EO startup has faced since the beginning of time. You have run this play twice. KSAT sold the data before the use case was fully proven by finding the most exposed buyer and running a pilot that created precedent. GFW sold the data by finding the buyers who needed a structured commercial relationship even though the free version was sufficient. Xoople is at the stage where they need to build the commercial function with intention, not hope. The question is whether you want to do it in Spain, for a company that is well-funded but unproven, or whether you would rather do it for a company that already has revenue and customers and just needs someone to scale what is working. If the answer is the former, Xoople is worth a conversation. If the answer is the latter, keep looking.

watch_list.why: Paris-based EO analytics for energy, methane, and climate markets. Sells to hedge funds, oil majors, governments. ENI-era Paul speaks this customer's language fluently.
watch_list.roast: Paris-based satellite data company that got famous catching Russian methane leaks from space and selling the footage to hedge funds. Their product pitch is 'environmental intelligence,' which is the business equivalent of being the DJ at a very expensive party where oil majors, sovereign wealth funds, and climate activists all pretend not to know each other. Your ENI days were basically the same room ,  European regulatory sophistication, corporate energy executives, satellite vendor in the corner closing the deal. You were that vendor. You speak their language, you survived French bureaucracy once, and you know which side of the methane ledger actually pays. Send a 'bonjour.'

watch_list.why: SF-based hyperspectral EO constellation for pipeline and infrastructure monitoring. Defense + commercial energy customers. BD role overlap with Paul's dual-use commercial experience.
watch_list.roast: California hyperspectral startup that stares at pipelines for a living. They detect methane leaks and pipeline corrosion from orbit, which is somehow less creepy than the Russian surveillance states but equally commercial. Backed by real DoD contracts and commercial energy majors, with a tech stack that reads like a Transformer's cousin. They need BD help to navigate between defense work and oil & gas deals without either customer flinching ,  which is exactly the split you learned to handle at KSAT when your Arctic ground stations served NOAA in the morning and the Norwegian military by afternoon. You can already talk 'monitoring' to an ExxonMobil VP without breaking eye contact. Go do it.

watch_list.why: Munich-based thermal infrared satellite constellation; 180+ employees; wildfire detection + climate intelligence; actively hiring commercial roles. Promoted distributed-sat pioneer Ignacio Zuleta to CTPO in Feb 2026. Direct KSAT-adjacent culture; European climate-tech customers.
watch_list.roast: Munich thermal intelligence shop that scaled from two satellites to fourteen in a single year, which is the NewSpace equivalent of a quiet kid coming back from summer camp six inches taller and suddenly fluent in French. They now operate more thermal IR satellites than any nation or company on Earth ,  including the United States, which is either an achievement, a DoD procurement scandal, or both depending on whose Congressional testimony you read next. Idaho signed them for statewide wildfire detection, Canada followed with a national early-warning contract, and every reinsurance CFO in the northern hemisphere is quietly doing the math on what a thirty-billion-dollar annual wildfire bill looks like when ignitions get spotted before the fire department's coffee cools. They elevated a distributed-satellite pioneer to CTPO in February, which is Silicon Valley code for 'the grown-ups are running product now and the commercial side is next.' Munich is a short Norwegian Air flight from Tromsø, your LinkedIn graph overlaps theirs by three degrees and a shared appreciation for dark beer, and thermal IR is the one EO niche where the customer literally cannot say no when their asset is already on fire. Pronounce it Oh-ROH-rah-Tek and skip the line before a Stanford MBA with a North Face jacket gets there first.

watch_list.why: Finnish SAR empire scaling to 50 sats/year by end-2026. NGA + NRO contracts, insurance + maritime + flood commercial side. Dual-use muscle Paul built at KSAT directly applies. Helsinki HQ, US BD in Virginia.
watch_list.roast: Finnish SAR empire that got rich selling radar pictures to the NRO, NGA, and every European intelligence agency with a checkbook. They're scaling to fifty satellites a year by end of 2026, which is the NewSpace equivalent of Henry Ford opening River Rouge ,  except the Model Ts see through clouds and cost forty million apiece. The commercial side is the part still looking for a BD adult: selling the same constellation to insurance giants, hedge funds, and flood-mapping customers who don't want to be in the same PowerPoint as a defense contract. That dual-use-without-freaking-out-civilians muscle is exactly what you built at KSAT, where NOAA scientists and Norwegian intelligence analysts shared a ground station and politely pretended not to see each other in the cafeteria. Helsinki is a short flight from Tromsø and they probably still have your old coffee order memorized.

watch_list.why: Belgian EO satellite manufacturer opening Megafactory (world's 3rd largest) in 2026 with new US manufacturing facility. Vertically integrated: builds constellation + sells imagery. US expansion means a US commercial leadership org is inevitable. Early-mover window for BD leadership.
watch_list.roast: Belgian satellite manufacturer opening a Megafactory in the US because Europe apparently couldn't contain their ambition. They build their own birds and sell the imagery, which makes them a rare vertically integrated player in a market otherwise crowded with 'we rent pixels from someone else' middlemen. Brussels bureaucracy forged them, which means they survived the same kind of byzantine EU procurement you endured at ENI ,  you two will have a lot to share over the sad conference coffee. The US expansion guarantees a US commercial org is coming, and whoever lands that seat gets to define the go-to-market for a constellation that isn't fully online yet. First-mover energy. Apply before some McKinsey alum with a French MBA and zero satellite data experience convinces the board otherwise.

watch_list.why: Finnish hyperspectral EO constellation; dual-use commercial + defense customers; ESA-aligned; KSAT-ecosystem adjacent. Commercial BD leadership window as constellation scales and CHIME-era European hyperspectral market comes online.
watch_list.roast: Finnish hyperspectral startup doing the thing hyperspectral startups do ,  selling 150 colors of Earth to whoever will listen, which turns out to be defense agencies, precision ag analytics firms, and the occasional hedge fund that wants to short soybeans three quarters early. The advantage of being Finnish is that the team ships actual product in temperatures that would make a Palo Alto founder write a Medium post about burnout, and they survived European commercial procurement without developing the learned helplessness most EU SaaS companies fall into. Your Tromsø-KSAT years speak fluent Nordic commercial, and there are maybe eight humans alive who can explain spectral unmixing to a mining executive without either party falling asleep. You're one of them. ESA's CHIME mission is about to blow open the European hyperspectral TAM, and Kuva is positioning as the commercial backbone. Watch for the US BD lead req when they expand this side of the Atlantic.

watch_list.why: Public NYSE:SPIR. 100+ sat constellation collecting weather, maritime (AIS), aviation (ADS-B). Global commercial org across Virginia/SF/Glasgow/Singapore. BD turnover is steady; KSAT-adjacent multi-vertical commercial muscle directly applies.
watch_list.roast: Public company with a stock chart that looks like a telemark wipeout on a sheet-ice day, except underneath the broken IPO and the Yahoo Finance comments section is an actual working global constellation of 100+ birds pulling weather, AIS, and ADS-B data that airlines, insurers, and navies keep paying for despite the equity narrative. Headquarters are in Virginia AND San Francisco AND Glasgow AND Singapore, which is code for 'we're somehow both a US defense contractor and a European climate shop,' which is the exact KSAT duality you spent a decade running. They're always hiring BD ,  turnover is Silicon Valley standard, the data is Brussels-class, and somebody has to sell forecasts to Delta while the engineers keep launching cubesats. Your satellite + enterprise background plus the fact that you'll actually take a 6am Oslo call makes you an unusually clean fit. The stock might still go to zero. The business might not. Those are two separate questions and you only need to care about the second one.

watch_list.why: Nasdaq-listed (SATL). Merlin constellation launching Oct 2026 promising daily 1m global remapping with new subscription-based persistent-monitoring model. Customer base spans defense, civil gov, commercial. Senior BD Manager roles surfacing. Commercial leadership hired a Maxar veteran who booked $1B in sales ,  middle-layer BD is the missing piece. Direct fit for Paul's dual-use commercial EO data licensing muscle.
watch_list.roast: Nasdaq-listed shop (ticker SATL) with Argentine founders, a Florida HQ, and the audacity to tell shareholders they'll remap the entire planet daily at one-meter resolution starting October 2026. The Merlin constellation is either the most ambitious EO play of the decade or a particularly elaborate quarterly earnings slide ,  pick your narrative based on whether you own the stock. The actual tech is stronger than the market cap suggests: vertically integrated birds, onboard AI, ten spectral bands, and a persistent-monitoring subscription model that finally abandons the 'we'll sell you a pixel' middle-school business plan for real recurring revenue. They hired a Maxar lifer who booked a billion in sales over seven years, which means the C-suite now knows what grown-up commercial looks like and they're desperately short of the middle-layer BD leaders who can actually close a ten-figure subscription pipeline before the next earnings call. Your KSAT years were this exact play ,  sell the capacity, sell the data, run dual-use without flinching at the procurement officer's third redline. Go see if they'll take a cold 'hei' from a gringo in Taos who already negotiated ENI down to a signed page and a tired handshake.

watch_list.why: Rebranded from Maxar Intelligence Oct 2025. Westminster CO (Mountain time zone). 10-bird constellation with 30cm daily global collection ,  largest commercial EO capacity on orbit. Announced Vantage (20cm) + Pulse (40cm) expansion April 2026. Dual-use customer base: NGA, NRO, DOD + commercial energy + conservation. Commercial BD org is massive and churning. KSAT dual-use muscle applies directly; watch for Director/VP Commercial roles as constellation expands.
watch_list.roast: Maxar rebranded as Vantor in October 2025, which is the corporate equivalent of getting a haircut after a bad divorce ,  same face, same satellites, fresh Instagram aesthetic. Westminster, Colorado, which is one Rocky Mountain weather system away from your Taos driveway and the same time zone as every coffee you've drunk since 2021. They operate ten imaging birds that already collect 30cm-resolution daily globally, which is more commercial pixel capacity than any competitor in the known universe, and in April 2026 they announced Vantage (20cm sun-sync) and Pulse (40cm mid-inclination smallsats) to double down before Iceye or Satellogic eats their lunch. The customer base is full KSAT flavor: NGA and NRO analysts in the morning, Amazon Conservation Team in the afternoon, urban planners and reinsurers in between, everyone politely pretending the defense work pays for the conservation work (it does). The BD org is enormous and always churning because every pixel licensee needs a grown-up who won't blink when the NRO asks for an exclusive or a mining major wants to redraft the licensing terms at 11pm on a Friday. You negotiated ENI for four years before the ink dried on a single paragraph. A Maxar-legacy procurement Zoom is lunch on a Tuesday. Watch their careers page like it owes you money.

watch_list.why: Munich/Freiburg/Brussels thermal intelligence constellation. Closed €37M Series A Feb 2026 (total raised €75M) led by Alpine Space Ventures + Lakestar. Defense-grade thermal IR for water stress, crop health, grid monitoring, plus national security customers. Actively hiring BD Manager for Earth Observation & Industrial Partnerships. KSAT dual-use muscle applies directly; European EO ecosystem Paul speaks fluently; commercial + defense customer mix is the exact work he did in Tromsø.
watch_list.roast: German thermal intelligence shop in Munich, Freiburg, Brussels, Washington and Toulouse that just pulled €37M Series A in February 2026, bringing total raised to €75M and suddenly making them the defense-grade thermal IR play Europe has been waiting to notice. Their pitch: build a thermal constellation that can tell the Bundeswehr which Russian tank is warm and the Spanish olive farmer which grove is thirsty, same week, same data pipeline, different portal. Alpine Space Ventures, Lakestar, Semapa Next, Bayern Kapital ,  real grownup capital, not the vibes-round money filling Series B press releases in Palo Alto. The BD req they posted ('Business Development Manager, Earth Observation & Industrial Partnerships') is literally your lane: commercial data licensing to agricultural analytics firms, water utilities, and reinsurance carriers, while the defense side runs on its own track with a separate procurement cycle and a different NDA stack. You've done this exact dual-use dance at KSAT for fifteen years ,  NOAA scientists in the morning, Norwegian intelligence in the afternoon, everyone in the cafeteria pretending not to see each other. Munich is a short hop from Tromsø, the culture reads KSAT-adjacent, and unlike half the US EO startups you've been chasing their constellation is already in orbit and their customers are already paying. Send the 'hallo' before some Berlin Wirtschaftsingenieur with an ESA fellowship and zero commercial scars gets there first.

watch_list.why: Berkeley-based CDR quality assurance provider founded 2023. Very early-stage (small team) but directly aligned with Paul's MRV + carbon market background. Watch for senior commercial hire as they raise Series A. Competitive with Isometric; adjacent to Sylvera and Frontier methodology.
watch_list.roast: Berkeley startup staffed like a PhD seminar and funded like a PhD seminar, roughly four people deciding whether everyone else in the carbon removal industry is lying about their tons. They're the self-appointed quality assurance layer for permanent removals, which is brave or insane or both ,  Isometric already claims that seat, Sylvera already rates the credits, and CDR buyers like Frontier and Stripe Climate write methodology protocols in-house. Absolute Climate's bet is that all three are doing it wrong and the industry needs one more opinionated referee. It's either going to get acquired by Stripe in eighteen months or disappear into academic PDFs cited twice and forgotten. At seed stage they don't have a BD seat yet, but when they raise an A the commercial lead will have to walk a carbon buyer through 'no, your ton is not a ton' without killing the sale, which is the exact negotiating muscle you built at ENI and at GFW when you told fishing ministers their fleet was visible and they didn't love hearing it. Watch this one like a hawk and pounce the week the senior hire announcement drops.

watch_list.why: Sydney + London climate risk modeler building on satellite data, ML, and climate-adjusted physics. Sells tropical cyclone, flood, wildfire models (DeepCyc flagship) to reinsurers and ILS investors. Enterprise SaaS with blue-chip buyer list (Lloyd's syndicates, Munich Re tier). Your satellite-plus-enterprise-data background and ENI long-contract experience translate directly. Remote-friendly given Sydney/London split.
watch_list.roast: Australian climate catastrophe modelers born out of UNSW physics labs, now selling tropical cyclone and flood risk to Lloyd's underwriters and reinsurance CFOs who've watched last season's hurricane bills break every model they had on the shelf. DeepCyc, their climate-adjusted cyclone product, is essentially 'what Moody's RMS should have been if it weren't still running 2003 peril math' ,  machine learning, synthetic event sets, actual climate adjustments built in, and a Sydney-and-London team that isn't trying to pass off a stationary climatology as 'vintage historical.' The insurance market is paying real money for this right now because the alternative is another Helene or Milton wiping out quarterly earnings on a pricing model built before Paul had a gray hair. Reask is small enough that a commercial leader walks into a defined lane and big enough that the customers on the call are blue-chip reinsurers with eight-figure budgets and patient legal teams. Your KSAT enterprise-data experience selling satellite products to government and corporate majors, plus the ENI years negotiating long contracts with counterparties who read every footnote, translate cleanly to reinsurance BD. Sydney is far. The Zoom work translates fine.

watch_list.why: London-based thermal-intelligence constellation. £30M ($40M) round Feb 2026 led by NATO Innovation Fund (total equity raised £60M). HotSat-2 and HotSat-3 launching 2026; HotSat-4/5 + long-lead HotSat-6 under contract. Dual-use commercial + defense customer base, KSAT-adjacent muscle. Watch for commercial leadership hires as constellation moves from one-bird demonstrator to nine-spacecraft operational.
watch_list.roast: London thermal-imaging shop that took £30M from the NATO Innovation Fund in February 2026 and now has the most defense-friendly cap table in commercial EO this side of Helsinki. Their 2023 demonstrator died in orbit, which would have killed most NewSpace startups outright but somehow the British just shrugged, raised again, and put HotSat-2 and HotSat-3 on the manifest for this year with HotSat-4 and HotSat-5 already under contract. The pitch is 'thermal infrared at meter-class resolution, ten to twenty daily revisits, and we'll tell your reinsurance underwriter exactly which warehouse roof in Lagos is leaking heat at 3am.' Customer mix runs the full KSAT-style dual-use spectrum: defense and intelligence agencies in the morning, energy majors and utilities in the afternoon, the reinsurance carriers writing checks before the fire season hits. Constellr in Munich is the obvious peer and OroraTech is the third corner ,  three thermal IR shops sorting themselves out in real time. SatVu is the British one with NATO money, ESA history, and a constellation that finally exists. Your KSAT decade is the closest analog on Earth to the commercial muscle they need next.

watch_list.why: Austin-based self-service Earth Intelligence platform brokering optical, SAR, hyperspectral, and aerial imagery from 50+ partners. Closed $12.7M Series A Jan 2026 (Buoyant Ventures + IronGate co-leads, DNV Ventures + J2 Ventures + RSquared). Selected April 2026 for NATO DIANA 2026 cohort. Hiring across government and defense team. Dual-use commercial + defense + civil. Direct fit for Paul's commercial EO data licensing experience.
watch_list.roast: Austin-based 'Uber for satellite imagery' that's closed $12.7M Series A in January 2026, then six weeks later got tapped for NATO's DIANA 2026 cohort ,  150 companies out of 3,600 applicants ,  which means they've now achieved the rare double of being shipped both venture money AND a defense innovation badge in the same quarter. The product is genuinely useful: a self-serve platform that brokers optical, SAR, hyperspectral, and aerial imagery from fifty-plus partners, lets a customer task a satellite from a credit card, and ships the file via API instead of a six-week purchase order and a procurement officer named Brad. The whole thing is what UrtheCast and DigitalGlobe should have built fifteen years ago instead of selling pixels through a wholesale channel that took longer to clear than the actual collection window. They're hiring across government and defense, which is one half of your KSAT muscle, and the commercial side has fifty data partners that all need someone who actually understands EO licensing economics ,  which is the other half. Buoyant Ventures and J2 Ventures wrote the checks; DNV Ventures came along which means there's a Norwegian thread you can casually pull on the first call. Austin is closer to Taos than half the Bay Area shops you've been chasing.

watch_list.why: Montreal-based methane and CO2 detection from space; 15-satellite operational constellation; launched two new satellites at COP30 in Belém November 2025. Customers span every supermajor and most regulators (Shell, ExxonMobil, BP, Chevron, Pemex, EDF, Carbon Mapper Coalition). Dual-customer model ,  sell the same data to operators and to regulators monitoring those operators ,  is direct KSAT lineage. Active hiring across analytics, science, BD; English-speaking Canadian HQ with EU presence. ENI-era methane work translates one-to-one.
watch_list.roast: Montreal methane shop that's been quietly building the most operationally serious greenhouse gas constellation on orbit while everyone else races to put the word 'methane' on a pitch deck. Fifteen satellites flying, two more launched at COP30 in Belém last November, and a customer list that reads like a hostile-witness deposition: Shell, ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP, Pemex, plus the Environmental Defense Fund standing in the corner reading the same readouts and writing the press releases. The pitch is brutal genius ,  they sell methane plume detection to the operators creating the leaks AND to the regulators trying to fine the operators creating the leaks, and somehow nobody's complained loudly enough to change the contract structure. California's Air Resources Board just used Tanager-1 to plug ten leaks in the same quarter, which means every state air board in the country is now budgeting for satellite methane data and GHGSat is the most operationally mature vendor on the call. Your KSAT methane work was exactly this game one degree of separation removed: convince a major to pay for satellite data that will eventually be used against them, do it in a tone the procurement officer can defend to the CFO, and never blink when the EDF policy lawyer asks for the same readout at half the price. The Quebec French is closer to Norwegian small talk than Silicon Valley pitch English, the customer rolodex is the same energy majors you negotiated against at ENI, and the only humans on Earth who can sell GHG monitoring to both an EDF advocacy lawyer AND an ExxonMobil VP without getting blown out of either room are people who built KSAT's commercial side from a wet circle in the Arctic. You're one. Apply before they hire a Stanford MBA whose 'hands-on methane experience' is a 2024 Sustainability Week panel.

watch_list.why: San Francisco / Bangalore climate forecasting platform serving ag, food, and finance. Series B $22M (Four Rivers Group, Radical Ventures, Yaletown, Neotribe) brought total to $38M. Customers include Dole, Driscoll's, Nuveen Natural Capital, Advanta Seeds. ARR grew 5x in the 18 months post-Series A. Commercial team scaling ,  direct fit for Paul's enterprise data licensing muscle (KSAT to ENI to GFW pattern). Bangalore presence means a Norwegian Air-friendly time zone overlap from Taos.
watch_list.roast: San Francisco climate forecasting outfit that sells Dole six-month-out predictions for what avocado prices will do when Mexican drought breaks the next heat record, and somehow has Driscoll's, Nuveen Natural Capital, and Advanta Seeds on the customer list paying real money for it. The pitch lives in the gap between weather forecasting (boring, commoditized, free) and climate scenarios (academic, wrong, useless to a procurement officer staring at a Q3 budget) ,  sub-seasonal probabilistic intelligence with measurable margin impact. They closed Series B around $22M led by Four Rivers Group with Radical Ventures and Yaletown along for the ride, bringing total raised to $38M, which is enough to fund a real commercial team and exactly the stage where the wrong VP of Sales hire torpedoes a company that could have been a category-defining win. ARR went up 5x in eighteen months post-Series A, which means the product works and the buyer cycle is shorter than the typical 'climate intelligence' Slack-thread death-march. The customer list looks like your KSAT enterprise rolodex with the cuisine swapped: ag majors instead of energy majors, food companies instead of fishing fleets, and procurement officers who all read the same Bloomberg piece on how climate volatility is going to eat their margins. Your KSAT enterprise muscle and the GFW commercialization scars translate cleanly: convince a Fortune 500 buyer that satellite-fueled forecasting is worth $250K/year while their CFO's first instinct is 'isn't this what AccuWeather does for free.' Bangalore presence means a Norwegian Air-friendly time zone overlap from Taos. Send a 'hello' before some Stanford MBA who's never sold a B2B subscription before convinces the CEO they can run commercial.

watch_list.why: Bremen-based, first satellite launching 2026, VHR optical + AI analytics, European defense angle fits KSAT/GFW pattern perfectly
watch_list.roast: Bremen startup with five million euros and a launch date. You have spent a decade explaining to oil majors why they should pay for imagery they did not know they needed, then another five years explaining to foundations why a free platform should also have a commercial program. Marble is the inverse bet. They are building the constellation before the buyer fully exists, betting European defense ministries will panic-buy sovereign EO capacity after watching US companies turn off the feed during Iran. The thesis is clean. Sovereignty sells when the alternative is a voluntary withhold notice from Planet Labs. You know the KSAT playbook. Sell the Norwegians first because they will pay to not depend on Maxar. Then sell the narrative that proactive monitoring becomes required, not optional. Marble has the timing advantage because the Iran imagery blackout just reminded every EU defense minister that dependency is a liability. They need a Director of Partnerships who understands how to translate a satellite into a regulatory argument. The risk is they are too early or the EU moves too slowly, and five million does not last long in the sat game. But if you are looking for a role where the entire commercial function is zero to one, and the pitch is write the requirement before the RFP exists, you have done this twice already. The difference is you would be doing it in Germany instead of Tromso, and for defense instead of oil spill. Not a perfect fit, but the bones are right.

watch_list.why: Bangalore, first multi-sensor satellite (SAR + optical on one platform) launching Q1 2026, 160kg, targeting insurance/defense/ag
watch_list.roast: Indian startup launching the first satellite that puts SAR and optical on the same bus, which sounds like the kind of technical elegance that makes an engineer happy and a commercial buyer confused. You spent three years at GFW trying to explain why someone would pay for vessel detection data that a free platform already shows. GalaxEye has the opposite problem. They have a genuinely differentiated technical product and now they need to find the buyer who understands why multi-sensor fusion matters before the competitor launches a simpler single-sensor product at half the cost. The market is insurance, defense, ag, infrastructure, which is startup-speak for we will sell to whoever answers the phone. You know this game. The technical capability is never the constraint. The constraint is finding the three verticals where multi-sensor data creates a decision advantage worth the contract price, then building the partnerships that make the buyer trust you before the data exists. Bangalore puts you twelve hours ahead of Taos, which is either a feature or a disaster depending on how you feel about morning calls with the UK and evening calls with California. The company has traction, the tech is legitimately differentiated, and they are at the stage where the commercial function needs to be built with intention instead of hope. The question is whether you want to build the India EO market from scratch, or whether you would rather do the same work for a company that already has European or US distribution. If the answer is the former, this is the role. If the answer is the latter, keep looking.

watch_list.why: Berlin-based, ESA-backed, building Twinspector constellation for infrastructure monitoring, secured seven-figure ESA funding April 2026
watch_list.roast: German company that convinced ESA to write a seven-figure check to build a dedicated constellation for infrastructure monitoring, which means somebody in Europe finally decided that proactive utility monitoring is a business instead of a science project. You know the pattern. KSAT sold Shell on proactive offshore monitoring by proving the regulator would eventually require it. LiveEO is running the same play but for power lines and pipelines on land instead of rigs at sea. The difference is the utility sector moves slower than oil majors, which is saying something. They have traction, they have ESA backing, and they just closed funding in April 2026, which means they are probably hiring for the next phase right now. The commercial challenge is identical to what you faced at KSAT in 2013. The technical capability is proven. The buyer knows they need it eventually. The question is how do you make eventually become now. You do that by finding the utility that has the most exposure, running a paid pilot that creates a precedent, then using that precedent to move the regulator. Once the regulator moves, every other utility has to buy or risk looking negligent. You have run this play twice. You could run it again for LiveEO, but this time for European utilities instead of Australian offshore oil. The role would be building the commercial function and the regulatory strategy in parallel, which is exactly the kind of market-making work you do well. The downside is Berlin is not Taos, and the utility sector is slower than oil and gas, which you may have finally lost patience for. But if the idea of getting paid to move a regulator appeals to you, the bones are right.

watch_list.why: Israeli startup using satellite data, climate models, and AI to build resilience platforms for land-based supply chains (agriculture, forestry). Direct EO application, climate-adjacent, mission-commercial tension Paul knows well.
watch_list.roast: Marvin is an AI copilot for resilient agricultural supply chains, which sounds like someone fed a venture pitch generator the words satellite, climate, and value chain and hit enter three times. The company is Israeli, the domain is hard, and the customer is an agribusiness trying to figure out whether a vegetation index means replant or wait two weeks. You spent a decade at KSAT convincing oil majors that proactive monitoring beats reactive crisis management. Marvin is selling the same argument to a different vertical, except the regulator is slower, the margin is thinner, and the buyer does not know what threshold triggers action. They raised money, they have a product, and someone will eventually figure out what the copilot is supposed to do when the model says the harvest fails in June. The opportunity is proving that the platform can sell to more than one crop, more than one country, and more than one skeptical procurement officer who has seen five satellite startups pitch this year. If you can translate KSAT's multi-vertical playbook into agricultural risk management, this is the kind of builder-stage infrastructure company you could actually help scale. If not, it is a really nice slide deck about the future of food systems.

watch_list.why: Converts biomass into carbon-rich liquid, injects it underground for permanent removal. Durability-focused, scaling commercially, direct climate impact with verifiable MRV. Paul's GFW commercial-mission framing applies cleanly.
watch_list.roast: Charm Industrial takes agricultural waste, turns it into bio-oil, and pumps it underground where it stays for thousands of years. It is one of the few carbon removal pathways that does not require you to pretend enhanced weathering is ready for prime time or that direct air capture pencils out without a hyperscaler writing the check. They have actual removal credits sold, actual injection sites operating, and actual customers who care whether the carbon stays buried or leaks back out in fifteen years. You built the Planet deal at GFW by knowing which buyers pay for verified outcomes instead of aspirational offsets. Charm is the same bet in a different market. The carbon credit world is a wasteland of consultants, registries, and startups that cannot explain their methodology without a whiteboard and twenty minutes. Charm is one of the few that can say we put it in the ground, here is the well, here is the monitoring, here is the third party attestation. The commercial challenge is not the science. It is whether enterprises actually allocate budget to durable removal or whether they keep buying cheap forestry credits and calling it net zero. If Paul can help them frame the value proposition so the CFO and the chief sustainability officer both nod, they win. If not, it is another breakthrough technology waiting for the market to catch up.

watch_list.why: Building satellite and radar-based extreme weather forecasting systems for agriculture, logistics, energy. High-frequency EO + AI, enterprise customers, operational decision-making use case. Adjacent to Paul's GFW real-time data experience.
watch_list.roast: Climavision is trying to make weather forecasting fast enough and accurate enough that someone will actually change their logistics route, delay a harvest, or shut down a transmission line before the storm hits instead of after. The company runs its own weather radars, ingests satellite data, wraps it in AI-driven prediction models, and sells subscriptions to industries that lose money every time they guess wrong about a thunderstorm. The pitch is solid. The technical execution is real. The business model is recurring revenue from enterprises that already budget for weather intelligence. The problem is the same problem every high-frequency EO company eventually hits. Buyers believe the data matters right up until they have to integrate it into an actual operational workflow, at which point someone in the organization says we already have NOAA for free and this costs how much per seat. You sold KSAT oil spill monitoring into a market that had no category and no budget line. Climavision is selling into a market that has both but assumes the free version is good enough. If Paul can help them articulate why sub-hour forecasting is worth enterprise budget and how to structure a pilot that proves ROI in the first 90 days, they have a shot. If not, it is another weather company with better models and no path to the decision maker who actually signs.

watch_list.why: Dutch EO startup using passive microwave sensing for soil moisture, vegetation, and weather insights. Agriculture, insurance, banking, commodities verticals. Established European player, commercially mature buyer base. Paul's GFW insurance work (AXA Climate, British Marine/QBE) is directly adjacent, and the multi-sector licensing model echoes KSAT's multi-vertical services.
watch_list.roast: VanderSat sells microwave-derived soil moisture and vegetation data to agriculture, insurance, banking, and commodities buyers, which is a polite way of saying they sell the same dataset to four different industries and call it four different products depending on who is asking. They have been at this since 2014, so they are not a moonshot, they are a functioning commercial operation with actual recurring customers, which in the EO world makes them a statistical anomaly. The microwave angle is clever because it works through cloud cover, which means they can monitor crops in the Netherlands in February, which is otherwise impossible and also depressing. The challenge is that they are in the most competitive segment of EO. Every agtech platform, every precision agriculture startup, and half the insurance industry has a satellite data partnership. VanderSat has survived a decade, so they clearly have something defensible, but Paul would need to understand what it is. Is it the microwave sensor advantage? Is it the algorithm? Or is it just that they were early and the customers have no reason to switch? If it is the third one, that is fine, but it means the growth story is about adding new verticals, not expanding within existing ones. Paul has done that. GFW's insurance vertical was net-new, built from a use case the buyers did not know they had. If VanderSat wants to find the fifth vertical, he has run that play.

watch_list.why: UK startup building climate risk reporting tools using satellite EO data for biodiversity, drought, heat stress, wildfire. Targets businesses, legislators, environmentalists. Aligns with evolving environmental laws. Paul's GFW conservation mission, open-access commercial tension, and Planet partnership (monitoring use case creation) are all relevant. UK-based, small team, mission-commercial balance.
watch_list.roast: Map Impact sells climate risk reporting tools built on satellite data, which sounds like every ESG analytics pitch from the last four years except they actually name the tools BiodiversityView, DroughtView, HeatView, WildfireView, like they are releasing a Marvel franchise. They are a UK startup, probably six people in a co-working space in Shoreditch, and they are trying to turn satellite-derived risk scores into something a compliance officer will pay for annually. The opportunity is real. EUDR is in force, CSRD is rolling out, and every corporation with a supply chain in a forest is suddenly very interested in whether that forest still exists. The risk is that Map Impact is one layer removed from the actual pain. They do not sell to the companies that own the land, they sell to the companies that report on the companies that own the land. That is a viable business, but it is a services business dressed up as a data business, and the margins reflect it. Paul has spent his whole career one step closer to the operational buyer, the person who actually has to make a decision based on the data. If Map Impact wants to move upstream and sell directly to the asset owner or the supply chain manager, that is a different motion, and Paul has done it. GFW built the ASN subsea cable deal by identifying a non-obvious use case for vessel detection data and then constructing the commercial argument from scratch. Map Impact has the data and the regulatory tailwind. The question is whether they can build the argument that makes the buyer feel like they have no choice but to pay.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based soil carbon MRV at 90% cost reduction. Profitable while competitors burn cash. 2M+ tonnes annually. Earthshot Prize winner. Global South focus.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil spill monitoring to super majors who could afford your day rate and you walked Planet's 1.2 billion hectare negotiation through three parties who each had their own commercial truth to protect. Boomitra is what happens when someone finally decides the entire voluntary carbon market has been designed backwards. They use satellites and AI to measure soil carbon for smallholder farmers in India, Argentina, Paraguay, places where the per-farm payment is measured in hundreds of dollars, not hundreds of thousands, and where traditional MRV would cost more than the carbon credit is worth. They claim 90% cost reduction. They registered South America's first grassland restoration project. They are profitable, which in the soil carbon space is like saying you found the Ark of the Covenant in a storage unit in Fresno. Most of their competitors are still trying to convince enterprises in Denver that regenerative ag is a line item, and they are out there issuing credits to people who have never heard the term additionality and do not care. The Technology is not the problem anymore, the problem is whether anyone actually believes the market will pay for soil carbon at scale, and Boomitra has a model that works whether the VCM fixes itself or not. They raised $4M, Yara Growth Ventures led it, and they secured $35M in project financing to cover five million acres across nine countries. That is not a pilot, that is a program. You have spent your entire career figuring out how to sell satellite data to the buyers no one thought would pay for it, how to structure deals when the obvious customer is not the right customer, and how to build commercial programs in spaces where the use case has to be explained before the product can be sold. Boomitra has done that in the most complicated corner of the carbon market and the question is whether you want to be part of the next phase or whether you want to watch someone else build the commercial licensing model you would have designed. They are in Menlo Park, which is not Taos, but they are fully remote, which is.

watch_list.why: Regenerative ag, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced weathering. Sells through Puro, Isometric, Verra, Gold Standard. $20M Series B first tranche Feb 2026, WestBridge Capital led, targeting $45M full round.
watch_list.roast: Varaha is what happens when someone finally admits that no single carbon removal pathway is going to save the voluntary carbon market and decides to build a portfolio instead of a religion. They do regenerative agriculture, agroforestry, biochar, enhanced rock weathering. They sell credits through Puro, Isometric, Verra, Gold Standard, and Carbon Standards International, which means they have figured out how to navigate five different verification regimes without setting their hair on fire, a skill you learned the hard way when you were trying to get KSAT into EMSA and AMSA at the same time and every regulator had their own version of what oil spill monitoring was supposed to mean. They raised $20M in their Series B first tranche in February, WestBridge Capital led it, they are targeting $45M for the full round, and they are based in Gurugram, India, which is a twelve-hour flight from Taos but a direct match for the complexity you have spent twenty years managing. The carbon markets right now feel like the EO sector in 2011, everyone knows something is about to happen, no one knows which business model will survive, and the companies that win are going to be the ones who can talk to buyers in five different languages and sell the same ton of carbon three different ways depending on who is signing the check. You built GFW's commercial licensing program by figuring out which derived products had private sector value on top of an open-access platform, and Varaha is doing the same thing but in reverse, they are figuring out which removal pathways have market traction and then building the MRV and verification stack to support whatever the buyer wants to hear. They are not remote, they are India-based, but they are scaling fast and they need someone who understands how to build the commercial function when the product is new, the market is skeptical, and the regulatory environment is a moving target. That person is you, or it is someone who learned everything you know by making your mistakes five years from now.

watch_list.why: Amsterdam-based. Combines satellite monitoring, field sensors, mobile apps. Tracks carbon, biodiversity, water benefits, and socioeconomic outcomes. Holistic MRV for agroforestry and reforestation.
watch_list.roast: Treecycle is an Amsterdam-based MRV platform for agroforestry and reforestation projects, and if you are wondering whether the world needs another forestry carbon MRV startup the answer is probably not, but if you are wondering whether the world needs one that actually tracks socioeconomic metrics alongside carbon sequestration the answer is yes and that is what Treecycle does. They combine satellite monitoring, field sensors, and mobile applications that local project managers actually use, which is a harder problem than it sounds because most MRV systems are designed by people in Berlin who have never tried to get a field manager in Tanzania to upload data on a phone with intermittent signal. They track carbon, biodiversity, water benefits, and community economic outcomes, which is the kind of holistic monitoring that makes NGOs weep with joy and makes corporate buyers nervous because it is harder to reduce to a single number on a slide. You spent three years building the Planet Labs deal for GFW, and the reason that deal worked is because you understood that the use case was not just small vessel detection, it was also proving that GFW could process 200 million hectares a day and still deliver outputs that passed scientific review, and Treecycle is doing the same thing but in the agroforestry space, they are proving that MRV can be comprehensive and still be affordable. They are European, which means they are dealing with a regulatory environment that actually cares about biodiversity co-benefits and does not treat carbon as the only metric that matters, and they are building a platform that works for projects that are too small for the Veras of the world to care about but too important to ignore. You have built commercial programs in spaces where the customer does not know they need the product yet and the product has to be explained before it can be sold. Treecycle is in that space right now. Amsterdam is not Taos, but it is closer than Tromso, and the work is the kind of thing you would design if someone gave you a blank sheet of paper and said build the MRV platform the carbon markets should have had ten years ago.

watch_list.why: Leading independent verifier for nature-based carbon, Amazon/Airbnb/Netflix customers, Gates + Lowercarbon backed
watch_list.roast: Pachama uses satellite imagery and machine learning to verify forest carbon projects. They do not plant the trees. They do not own the land. They just tell you whether the carbon everyone else is claiming is actually there. That makes them the referees in a game where half the players are accused of cheating and the other half are trying to prove they are not. Their customer list is Amazon, Airbnb, Netflix, and Nespresso. Their investor list is Bill Gates' BEV, Chris Sacca's Lowercarbon, and Amazon Climate Pledge. That is the kind of backing that makes them the default verification vendor for anyone who does not want to get burned buying forest offsets. The market only works if the credits are real, and Pachama has become the leading independent answer to that question. Paul's entire KSAT career was built on selling the commercial argument that proactive monitoring creates value even when no one wants to admit they need it yet. Pachama is doing the same thing but in carbon markets instead of oil spills. The difference is the voluntary carbon market is a mess of competing standards, questionable baselines, and buyers who got burned in 2023 when half the credits they bought turned out to be hot air. Pachama is the MDA of forest carbon except they are platform-agnostic and their competitive moat is trust, not hardware. Paul has negotiated with Planet, Airbus, MDA, and Maxar. He understands how imagery providers price into enterprise deals and how verification businesses build margin on top of someone else's data. Pachama is fully remote, mission-driven, and backed by people who will fund patient growth. The job would be building the commercial function for the company that everyone in carbon has to use but no one wants to admit they are paying for. That is Paul's entire professional biography in one sentence.

haiku:
Lomu in round one\
Utah tackle plugs the line\
the trench gets adult

One percent snowpack\
Sangres east drained out by March\
the river runs thin

Palo Flechado\
four percent of average\
the ditches still wait

Acequias dry\
rationing in Talpa now\
fire damage still rules

Artemis returns\
four around the moon and home\
fifty years between

Ten deals took the cake\
twenty-eight percent of all\
late-stage eats the rest

Brussels punts again\
EUDR slides a year\
pilots get their breath

Mud splatters the truck\
April rolls into a May\
that looks like July

Cast wider, he said\
the net pulls in stranger fish\
breadth before the bite

Corn snow at sunrise\
spring lines hold above tree line\
dawn patrol still pays

Viasat goes up\
Falcon Heavy on the Cape\
twenty-seventh dusk

Secret Service swept
the dinner, but the inbox stays
quiet in Taos sun

Sox won on Patriots Day
eight runs, zero homers, grit
Cora shrugs, moves on

Svalbard ten days warm
above zero in April
KSAT saw it first

Planet shuts the feed
government asks, image stops
open data dies

G7 in Paris
climate left off the agenda
to keep Trump at table

Red Willow Park trail
closed one day this week for work
the gorge never closes

Correspondents fled
bulletproof vest held the line
haiku holds the shift

Food systems at brink
UN warns, heat redefines yield
mesa dust agrees

ATS black hole swallows
five tailored cover letters
haiku make it through

The wire stays quiet
April mud, April waiting
Wheeler holds the snow

Redhead at the bar
asked what I do for living
satellite silence

Sox fewest homers
American League basement
still beat the Tigers

Strait of Hormuz closed
oil at ninety, Sox at nine
each leak costs something

Trump scrapped Pakistan
Iran talks move to the phone
too much time wasted

Gas four eleven
fifth straight day climbing the wire
fossils still have teeth

Sub twenty minutes
the new tactical standard
BlackSky Gen Three lives

Sox won Marathon
Monday, eight to six, traffic
Narvaez stole third slow

Nine and sixteen now
small ball, no power, walks, grit
Yankees arrive Tuesday

Red Willow trail closed
April twenty seventh, Taos
temporary, spring work

Kachina shut March
twenty ninth, warm winter, base
seventeen inches gone

Late April haiku
the wire is quiet, sage blooms
gorge holds the long light

Monsoon two months out
mud season ends, Wheeler dries
Taos in between

Gas at four thirty
Strait of Hormuz still shuttered
the wire still waits

Sox eight and thirteen
Patriots Day comeback win
Taos remembers too

Mortgage rates climbing
war premium on the thirty-year
still cheaper than rent

Planet imagery
restricted over Iran
Chinese sats still shoot

Caterpillar pops ten
S and P hits all-time high
the inbox stays quiet

Colombia summit
fifty nations ditch fossil fuels
US not invited

Emperor penguins
endangered by melting ice
we sold Shell the spill maps

April turns to May
Oskar graduates in weeks
Aurlandsdalen waits

Late April in Taos
mud season shifts to dust season
either way it's honest

May Day protests swell
Charlotte schools closed by teachers
The wire still burns

Gas hits four thirty
Iran talks through Pakistan
Taos sage holds still

Sox start nine and thirteen
no power in the lineup
Rafaela bunts home

Patriots Day win
eight to six on small ball grit
Ortiz voice still echoes

Supreme Court cuts maps
Louisiana's second lost
Voting Rights Act bled

MethaneSAT dark still
a billion-dollar corpse
no one claims the body

Smokey Bear turns eighty
four-pound cub in a shoebox
Capitan parades

Mortgage rate six three
Fed holds, the market shrugs
First-time buyers wait

Roblox slashed guidance
stock drops twenty-four percent
Market shrugs, moves on

Taos high seventy
eleven hours of bright sun
mud season ending

NISAR launched July
India and NASA trade
centimeter ground truth

Redhead at the bar
two drinks in, talks soil carbon
I should walk over

Ohio Senate race
Sherrod Brown returns to run
primary tonight

Strait of Hormuz closed
Trump sends navy Project Free
oil climbs at dawn

Voter roll purge push
ninety day window opens
DOJ brings the wire

SpaceX launched Sunday
forty five satellites up
EarthDaily rides north

Red Sox eight to six
Rafaela two run single
Patriots Day win

Gerrymandering
Supreme Court chips Voting Rights
twenty seats in play

Wind and solar first
thirty percent EU power
fossils at twenty nine

Planet three Pelicans
ICEYE two more eyes up
the constellation grows

Bruins season done
Sabres four to one game six
Pastrnak goes silent

Senate toss up math
Alaska is the maker
fifty fifty wire

Casas sixty day
abdominal strain shutdown
May drags on without
